quotes.text: I had my fortune; 'twasn't for the want of a roof over my head that I came here.
quotes.speaker: Mena Glavin
quotes.context: Mena to Nanna, defending her place in the household and the dowry she brought.

quotes.text: A cluckin' hen won't hatch!
quotes.speaker: Mena Glavin
quotes.context: Nanna's parting shot at Mena's childlessness, expressing the social value placed on women's fertility in this community.

quotes.text: Saints preserve us! Out working with a farmer you should be, my girl, instead of getting your head filled with high notions.
quotes.speaker: Mena Glavin
quotes.context: Mena lashing out at Sive for attending the convent school instead of earning.

quotes.text: You are a bye-child, a common bye-child, a bastard!
quotes.speaker: Mena Glavin
quotes.context: Mena cornering Sive, weaponising her illegitimacy to break her resistance to the match.

quotes.text: You will have no name till you take a husband.
quotes.speaker: Mena Glavin
quotes.context: Mena to Sive, insisting that an illegitimate woman has no social existence except through marriage.

quotes.text: I will open you! I will open you if you vex me more.
quotes.speaker: Mena Glavin
quotes.context: Mena threatening Nanna with a knife. The moment domestic tyranny tips into physical violence.

quotes.text: And I would be rid of her. And 200 pounds into the bargain.
quotes.speaker: Mena Glavin
quotes.context: Mena, calculating, the moment greed and resentment fuse into the decision to sell Sive.

quotes.text: Think of the 200 sovereigns dancing in the heel of your fist.
quotes.speaker: Thomasheen Sean Rua
quotes.context: Thomasheen pressing Mena to consider the cash, the matchmaker as tempter.

quotes.text: Tis the heat before death that plays upon them.
quotes.speaker: Thomasheen Sean Rua
quotes.context: Thomasheen explaining old men's appetite for young women, defending the proposed match.

quotes.text: You must go to the core of the apple to come by the seed.
quotes.speaker: Thomasheen Sean Rua
quotes.context: Thomasheen to himself, satisfied that he has read Mena correctly. The matchmaker's working philosophy.

quotes.text: Ah, you have no life, God help us, with the worry of them.
quotes.speaker: Thomasheen Sean Rua
quotes.context: Thomasheen flattering Mena about the burden of Sive and Nanna, building the sale.

quotes.text: Never! ... if the sun, moon and stars rained down out of the heavens and split the ground under my feet ... never!
quotes.speaker: Mike Glavin
quotes.context: Mike's first reaction to the proposed match. He will not hold this position.

quotes.text: It would be like tossing the white flower of the canavaun on to the manure heap.
quotes.speaker: Mike Glavin
quotes.context: Mike refusing the match, in the play's most lyrical line about Sive's purity.

quotes.text: Quick is everything. Quick is marriage and quick is love and quick is youth.
quotes.speaker: Mike Glavin
quotes.context: Mike rationalising the match to Nanna, his rhetoric collapsing as he persuades himself.

quotes.text: There is a hatchery of sin in this house.
quotes.speaker: Nanna Glavin
quotes.context: Nanna's repeated condemnation of the household's moral state. The line returns at the curtain.

quotes.text: How is it all men will find words to save themselves? Women must pay for all happiness.
quotes.speaker: Nanna Glavin
quotes.context: Nanna to Mike, the play's clearest articulation of its position on gender.

quotes.text: He frightened the life out of me. I never expected it!
quotes.speaker: Sive
quotes.context: Sive to Nanna after Sean Dota's attempt to assault her on the road. The first explicit sign of the danger she is in.

quotes.text: I could never live with that old man.
quotes.speaker: Sive
quotes.context: Sive to Mena, refusing the match outright before Mena's coercion overwhelms her.

quotes.text: Imagine going to a dance with him, or going up the chapel with him!
quotes.speaker: Sive
quotes.context: Sive trying to convey to Mena how impossible the match feels in the simple terms of public life.

quotes.text: The tubes of the bicycle are full of holes.
quotes.speaker: Sive
quotes.context: Sive's last attempt at ordinary teenage conversation before Mena overwhelms her with the marriage plan.

quotes.text: I would marry nobody but you, Sive, I love you. How would I marry anybody but you!
quotes.speaker: Liam Scuab
quotes.context: Liam's first declaration to Sive. Plainspoken, unmediated by any of the play's transactional framing.

quotes.text: You killed her! ... you ... you ... you killed her! You horrible filthy bitch!
quotes.speaker: Liam Scuab
quotes.context: Liam, having carried Sive's drowned body in, denouncing Mena directly at the climax.

quotes.text: What is it about Sive?
quotes.speaker: Mike Glavin
quotes.context: Mike's first puzzled response when Mena begins to introduce the proposal. The moment before he understands what is being asked of him.

quotes.text: Tis only what I see in my travels.
quotes.speaker: Pats Bocock
quotes.context: Pats's refrain. Positions the tinker-poet as outside the community, reporting on it. The chorus's epistemic stance.

quotes.text: There is no luck in refusing a man of the road!
quotes.speaker: Pats Bocock
quotes.context: Pats invoking traveller code when Thomasheen refuses to give alms. Casts the curse-song that follows as cosmic justice rather than spite.

quotes.text: Oh! Mike Glavin you're the man, you was always in the van
quotes.speaker: Carthalawn (singing)
quotes.context: Opening line of Carthalawn's praise-song for the man of the house, the ritual greeting on entering. The chorus's first appearance, sung formally, sets up the contrast with the curse-songs that follow.

quotes.text: Oh, come all good men and true, A sad tale I'll tell to you, All of a maiden fair, who died this day
quotes.speaker: Carthalawn (singing)
quotes.context: Opening lines of the play's closing lament. The chorus delivers the final verdict: Sive was murdered, not lost.

quotes.text: Oh, they murdered lovely Sive, She would not be a bride
quotes.speaker: Carthalawn (singing)
quotes.context: Carthalawn's revised closing line in the lament's final verse, where the soft 'drowned' of the first verse is replaced by 'murdered'. The chorus's moral verdict.

quotes.text: Sean Dota is the man.
quotes.speaker: Thomasheen Sean Rua
quotes.context: Thomasheen naming the suitor for the first time. The moment Sive's fate is shaped.

quotes.text: Will she walk the road? ... Far from it.
quotes.speaker: Mena Glavin
quotes.context: Mena gloating that Nanna will not actually leave despite her threats. The County Home as the unspoken alternative for old women without sons.

quotes.text: If there is a wedding, 'twill be on the morning of the wedding, on the word of a man.
quotes.speaker: Thomasheen Sean Rua
quotes.context: Thomasheen confirming when the money will change hands. The transaction made fully explicit.

quotes.text: Give me the letter!
quotes.speaker: Mike Glavin
quotes.context: Mike, having argued with Nanna, agrees to deliver Liam's letter to Sive. The brief moment in which Mike's conscience could have saved her.

quotes.text: Leave it burn!
quotes.speaker: Thomasheen Sean Rua
quotes.context: Thomasheen burning Liam's letter in front of Mike. The play's structural hinge: the rescue plan destroyed without Sive ever knowing it existed.
